result:
Prompt caching lets an LLM API reuse the internal computation (attention key/value states) from a previous request when a later request shares an identical prefix of input tokens — typically a long system prompt, tool definitions, or document context that stays constant across calls. Instead of reprocessing that prefix from scratch, the model reads it from a cache, which cuts both latency and cost for the repeated portion.

Cached tokens are billed at a discount because they save real compute: the provider skips the expensive forward-pass work of encoding those tokens again, so the cost savings are passed through (often 90% cheaper for cache "reads," though there's usually a small premium to write to the cache initially).

The practical thing to do: structure your prompts so the stable, reusable content (system instructions, tool schemas, few-shot examples, large reference documents) comes first, and the variable, per-request content (the user's actual question) comes last. Caches key off exact prefix matches, so putting volatile content early invalidates the cache for everything after it.

★ Insight ─────────────────────────────────────
Cache invalidation is prefix-based, not content-based — changing even one token early in the prompt breaks the cache for the entire remainder, which is why ordering (stable-then-variable) matters more than total prompt size.
Caches also expire quickly (often ~5 minutes of inactivity), so caching pays off most for bursty, repeated calls against the same context (agents looping, chat sessions) rather than isolated one-off requests.
─────────────────────────────────────────────────
